narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, based on factors like grades, test scores, and background. For you, it estimated a 48.2% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that triggered the rejection—meaning the school sees this as a risk to their pass rates. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to peers, signaling to the model a potential struggle on high-stakes exams like the bar. On a brighter note, your LSAT score, slightly below the typical student's but still competitive, strongly pulled the prediction in the opposite direction, toward expecting you to pass. Your undergraduate GPA, around 2.8 versus the average of over 3.2, also helped by nudging things toward success, as did your high family income bracket, which often correlates with better resources and outcomes in the data. Your Year 1 decile in the bottom group added a smaller push toward the failure side, while planning to attend full-time— like most students—barely influenced the decision. Compared to typical applicants, your academic deciles stand out as lower than average, while your LSAT and background strengths align more closely with those who succeed. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a brilliant Black woman ready to advocate for justice—models like this capture patterns but can't see your full story, resilience, or growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that strong influence even higher, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or programs, and highlighting any personal achievements or support systems in a new application. Many have turned similar predictions around by addressing these key areas— you've got this, and reapplying with improvements could change the outcome next time. Keep pushing; your voice is needed in law.
